messages.content:
You are a validator checking whether a PTSD patient's self-report profile is clinically coherent.

Demographics and trauma type have already been validated. Your job is to check two things:
1. Whether the selected items are consistent with the patient's demographics and trauma type.
2. Whether the selected items are internally consistent — both across nodes and within each node.

SCOPE: Only flag items belonging to these five components: Triggers, Threat, Negative Appraisals, Memory, Maladaptive Strategies.
Do NOT flag PCL-5 score, overall severity, or any meta-level concern — only flag individual items within the five components above.

JUDGMENT GUIDELINES:

- Clearly implausible vs merely unusual:
  A combination is clearly implausible if a clinician would immediately question whether the items could describe the same patient with the same trauma.
  Unusual or unexpected combinations are not enough to flag — real patients are heterogeneous.
  Only flag when the mismatch is hard to explain clinically.
  If you can construct any plausible clinical narrative that connects the item to the trauma type, do NOT flag it — even if that narrative requires some inference.

- Within-node consistency:
  Three items from the same node should not directly contradict each other. 
  Thematic redundancy is fine. Flag only when two items within the same node express opposing clinical states — for example, easy verbal recall alongside complete inability to verbalize the event, or active rumination alongside total emotional numbness toward the event.

- Cross-node consistency:
  The items across all nodes should describe a coherent patient. 
  Flag when items from different nodes pull in opposite directions in a way that cannot plausibly coexist — for example, triggers that imply a specific threat context that is entirely absent from the appraisals and memory items, or maladaptive strategies that address a symptom type not represented anywhere else in the profile.

- PCL-5 severity:
  The PCL-5 score reflects overall symptom severity. 
  Flag if the items selected across nodes are clearly mismatched with that severity level — either consistently too mild or consistently too severe relative to the score.

Validate the following patient's self-report for clinical coherence with their demographics and trauma type.

Patient demographics:
  age: 66
  gender: Female
  nationality: South asian
  relationship_status: Widowed
  trauma_type: Terrorism
  pcl5: 38

Self-report items:
  Triggers: Crash Discussions: Talk about accidents, Auditory (Babies): Babies, Self-Consciousness: Being seen
  Maladaptive Strategies: Alcohol Use: drink alcohol, Avoid Eye Contact: look down, Stay Near Exit: make sure to stay close to exit
  Threat: Physical Reactivity: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience, Flashbacks: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again, Startle Response: Feeling jumpy or easily startled
  Memory: Visual Memories: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images., Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now., Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
  Negative Appraisals: Self-Doubt: I can't trust that I will do the right thing, Fear of Harm: You can never know who will harm you, Lost Emotions: I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

issues.explanation: For a terrorism-related trauma, accident discussions can be a plausible but indirect cue (e.g., hearing about crashes in a heightened threat context), so this is not clearly incoherent enough to flag.

No clear contradiction with terrorism trauma; babies/children can be emotionally salient triggers in many trauma survivors, especially if the event involved protecting others or loss in a public setting.
This is compatible with avoidance/hypervigilance and social discomfort after trauma; not internally contradictory.
Alcohol use is a common coping strategy in PTSD and can coexist with the other avoidance strategies listed.
Consistent with avoidance, shame, or hyperarousal; coherent with the rest of the profile.

issues.item: Stay Near Exit: make sure to stay close to exit
issues.explanation: This fits threat monitoring and escape-oriented avoidance, which are coherent with the threat and trigger items.

issues.item: Physical Reactivity: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
issues.explanation: Coherent with the trigger and memory items; no contradiction.

issues.item: Flashbacks: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
issues.explanation: Coherent with the reliving and photographic-memory items.

issues.item: Startle Response: Feeling jumpy or easily startled
issues.explanation: Fits hyperarousal and is internally consistent with the rest of the threat symptoms.

issues.item: Visual Memories: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
issues.explanation: Compatible with vivid trauma recall; no internal conflict with the other memory items.

issues.item: Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
issues.explanation: Consistent with flashbacks and intrusive reliving.

issues.item: Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
issues.explanation: This is essentially redundant with visual memories and reliving, but not contradictory.

issues.item: Self-Doubt: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
issues.explanation: Plausible post-trauma negative appraisal and consistent with avoidance/safety behaviors.

issues.item: Fear of Harm: You can never know who will harm you
issues.explanation: Highly coherent with terrorism trauma and hypervigilance.

issues.item: Lost Emotions: I will never be able to feel normal emotions again
issues.explanation: Compatible with chronic PTSD-related emotional numbing/detachment; not contradictory.
